does: This paper presents CPD Online, a detector for optimization-based adversarial suffixes that jailbreak large language models while maintaining fluency. The core innovation frames adversarial suffix detection as an online change-point detection problem over token-level next-token entropy streams. The method uses the LLM system prompt to establish a robust baseline entropy, standardizes user-token entropies, and applies a one-sided CUSUM statistic to identify when entropy patterns shift abnormally. CPD is model-agnostic, requires no training, operates online during inference, and localizes where adversarial suffixes begin. Evaluated on 1,012 optimization-based attacks across five methods (GCG, AutoDAN, AdvPrompter, BEAST, AutoDAN-HGA) and 1,012 benign perplexity-controlled prompts, CPD improves F1 scores over windowed-perplexity baselines across six open-weight models, achieving AUROC 0.88 and F1 0.82 on LLaMA-2-7B, and concentrating 79.6% of detection triggers inside adversarial suffixes versus 17-46% for perplexity methods.
connects: This work builds on the adversarial robustness literature for LLMs, particularly responding to recent optimization-based jailbreaking methods like Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) attacks that generate fluent suffixes defeating traditional static defenses. It extends the shift from static perplexity-based anomaly detection to more sensitive dynamic metrics, drawing on change-point detection theory from sequential analysis and anomaly detection literature. The paper connects to prior work on entropy-based model monitoring and behavioral anomaly detection, adapting classical CUSUM statistical methods from industrial quality control and cybersecurity to the language model domain. The approach also relates to prompt injection detection research but distinguishes itself by targeting the specific structure of optimization-based adversarial suffixes rather than generic malicious inputs, addressing the gap left by perplexity detectors that miss fluent attacks.
enables: This work enables real-time defense mechanisms against adversarial jailbreaks without expensive retraining or external classifiers, opening deployment on resource-constrained systems. The model-agnostic and training-free properties allow seamless integration into existing LLM inference pipelines. The online nature and localization capability suggest extensions to dynamic defense strategies that could adaptively respond when adversarial suffixes are detected mid-generation. Future work could explore combining CPD with other entropy-based metrics or developing adversarial suffix generators aware of entropy signatures to evaluate robustness. The framework also points toward broader applications in detecting other prompt-level anomalies or distributional shifts in input processing. Additionally, the approach could inspire research into entropy-based interpretability tools, understanding why optimization-based attacks produce characteristic entropy signatures, and developing provably robust defense mechanisms grounded in information-theoretic properties of adversarial prompts.
